title: Nephrolithiasis
oneLiner: Renal colic from ureteric stone; common in young adults; high morbidity from pain ± obstruction ± infection.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Obstructive pyelonephritis / pyonephrosis** – obstructing stone + infection → urosepsis + renal destruction. Key finding: fever ≥38°C + rigors + unilateral flank pain; urine dip: nitrite+ / leukocyte+; lactate rising. **THIS is the emergency – needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy or stent) not just antibiotics.**
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)** – can present with flank/back pain mimicking renal colic. Key finding: pulsatile abdominal mass + hypotension + shock; no haematuria; pain radiates to back. **If >65y or vascular risk, do bedside US or CT aorta before assuming stone.**
- **Bilateral obstructing stones / solitary kidney with anuria** – complete blockage → hyperkalaemia, acidosis, acute kidney injury. Key finding: anuria + rising creatinine + no visible stone on CT? Actually, CT will show obstruction. Urgent decompression needed to prevent permanent loss of function.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Acute onset severe unilateral flank/loin pain radiating to groin/testis/labia; patient restless, unable to sit still; nausea/vomiting; urgency/frequency/dysuria if stone near vesicoureteric junction; macroscopic haematuria.
- **Key exam findings:** Patient in distress, writhing; costovertebral angle tenderness; no peritonism; may have low-grade fever (<38°C) from inflammation; if fever >38°C, suspect infection.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Renal colic (ureteric stone)** – classic radiation, haematuria, CT confirms stone.
- **Pyelonephritis** – fever >38.5°C, rigors, positive urine culture, bilateral symptoms less common; imaging may show hydronephrosis but no stone.
- **Ruptured AAA** – older age, vascular risk factors, pulsatile mass, hypovolaemic shock, no haematuria.
- **Testicular torsion** – scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis; testicular ultrasound.
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest or back pain, pulse deficit, hypertension, widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Diverticulitis** – left lower quadrant pain, fever, change in bowel habit; CT shows diverticular inflammation.
- **Biliary colic/cholecystitis** – right upper quadrant pain, Murphy's sign, gallstones on ultrasound.
- **Appendicitis** – right iliac fossa pain, migration, anorexia; CT shows enlarged appendix.
- **Musculoskeletal back pain** – no autonomic features, no radiation to groin, no haematuria; movement-exacerbated.
- **Splitter:** **Haematuria** – if present on dipstick, strongly favours stone; if absent, think AAA or musculoskeletal cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urine dipstick (blood, nitrite, leukocytes, pH); pregnancy test in women of childbearing age; ECG if atypical chest pain or elderly.
- **Bloods:** U&E (creatinine, eGFR), CRP, FBC, calcium, urate; lactate if sepsis suspected; blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT KUB (low-dose non-contrast) – gold standard; detects nearly all stones, measures stone size, identifies hydronephrosis.
  - **Alternative (pregnant or young female):** Ultrasound KUB + plain AXR – less sensitive but avoids radiation; can show hydronephrosis and stones >5mm on XR.
  - **Second-line:** CT with IV contrast if CT KUB inconclusive or need to assess collecting system anatomy.
- **Special tests:** Stone analysis if passed (infrared spectroscopy); 24h urine collection for metabolic workup in recurrent stones or high-risk patients.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system used in UK; clinical diagnosis + CT confirmation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in all women of childbearing age (changes imaging and management); **blood cultures** before antibiotics if febrile (urosepsis).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Severe pain: Morphine 5-10mg IV/IM (or [check local policy] IV paracetamol + NSAID if no contraindication) until comfortable.
  - Nausea: Ondansetron 4-8mg IV/IM / metoclopramide 10mg IV/IM.
  - IV fluids if dehydrated, vomiting, or oliguric: 0.9% sodium chloride 500mL-1L over 1-2h.
  - Check renal function; if normal and no infection, can plan discharge with oral analgesia and tamsulosin (if stone >5mm in distal ureter).
  - If febrile or signs of sepsis: start empirical antibiotics (broad spectrum: e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or gentamicin [check local policy] after blood cultures) and call urology for urgent decompression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Admit if: sepsis, AKI, solitary kidney, uncontrolled pain, bilateral stones, large stone (>10mm) unlikely to pass, or social issues. Manage pain with PRN IV/oral analgesia; continue IV fluids if needed; monitor urine output; arrange urology review for potential ureteroscopy or ESWL.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Metabolic workup if recurrent or risk factors (see below). Dietary advice: increase fluid intake to produce >2L urine/day; reduce salt, moderate protein, avoid high oxalate foods if calcium oxalate stones (spinach, rhubarb, nuts, chocolate). Specific therapy based on stone type (e.g., thiazide for hypercalciuria, allopurinol for uric acid stones).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h unless on fluid restriction; VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20mg SC daily (if eGFR >30 and weight >50kg; adjust if obese or CKD); NBM if planned for surgical intervention; observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; strict fluid balance chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Pain:** Ibuprofen 400-600mg PO QDS (max 2.4g/day) – preferred if no contraindication (renal impairment, GI bleed, hypovolaemia). Monitor renal function.
  - **Severe pain:** Morphine sulphate 5-10mg IV over 5 minutes, then titrate; or oral morphine solution 10-20mg 4-hourly PRN.
  - **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4-8mg PO/IV TDS (max 24mg/day); or Metoclopramide 10mg PO/IV TDS.
  - **Tamsulosin** 400mcg PO once daily (for stone expulsion in distal ureter stones >5mm; use for up to 28 days; caution in hypotension, cataract surgery).
  - **Empirical antibiotics (if infection):** Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS (adjust if creatinine clearance <30) +/- Gentamicin [check local policy] (once daily dosing, check levels according to local protocol).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30, active peptic ulcer, coagulopathy, hypovolaemia. Tamsulosin risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome; known orthostatic hypotension.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** NSAIDs contraindicated in significant CKD; morphine reduce dose if eGFR <30; tamsulosin no renal adjustment but caution if hepatic impairment; gentamicin requires therapeutic drug monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin** – missing levels before second dose can cause nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity. Always check trough level (pre-dose) for once-daily dosing. **Morphine** – giving IV opioid without checking respiratory rate (<12/min) or GCS (risk of respiratory depression). **NSAIDs** – prescribing in hypovolaemia or AKI (no urine output) precipitates acute tubular necrosis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Obstructive pyelonephritis / pyonephrosis (sepsis, shock)
  - Renal colic uncontrolled pain
  - Acute kidney injury (bilateral or solitary kidney)
  - Ureteric perforation or rupture (rare, from instrumentation or large stone)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hydronephrosis / renal atrophy (if prolonged untreated obstruction)
  - Recurrent stones (up to 50% recur within 5-10 years)
  - Chronic kidney disease (esp. staghorn calculi, recurrent obstruction, or infection)
sections.Prognosis: - Majority (>90%) of stones <5mm pass spontaneously within 4-6 weeks; 10-20% require intervention. Mortality rare except from sepsis (urosepsis carries 20-40% mortality if treatment delayed). Recurrence high without preventive measures.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send stone for analysis** if patient passes or retrieves it – directs long-term prevention.
- **In febrile patient with obstructing stone, call urology within 1 hour** for emergency decompression – do not wait for antibiotics to work.
- **Advise patients to strain urine** to catch stone for analysis and to confirm passage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give NSAIDs if serum creatinine not checked** – reversible AKI from combined hypovolaemia + NSAID.
- **Do not discharge patient with fever or obstruction** – risk of rapidly progressing to septic shock.
- **Do not forget pregnancy test** – CT KUB is contraindicated in pregnancy; use ultrasound + limited KUB instead.
- **Do not give tamsulosin for stones <5mm** – no proven benefit; side effects outweigh.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Uncontrolled pain despite oral analgesia; vomiting causing dehydration; fever or rigors (infection); unable to pass urine (anuria); visible blood in urine with clots.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1-2 weeks if stone not passed; urology clinic referral if stone >5mm, remains in situ >4 weeks, or patient has recurrent stones. Arrange imaging (KUB or ultrasound) to confirm stone passage in 4-6 weeks if not visible.
sections.Escalation Criteria: - Call senior/ICU if: **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single parameter scoring 3 (esp. respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, SBP <90, heart rate >130, GCS <13). **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with fever suggests sepsis. **Anuria >12 hours** – call urology for emergency decompression. **Creatinine rising >50% from baseline** or >300 µmol/L – consider AKI and need for nephrostomy. **Crash team** if cardiac arrest or unresponsive/sepsis with refractory hypotension.

rawMarkdown:
## Nephrolithiasis
**One-liner:** Renal colic from ureteric stone; common in young adults; high morbidity from pain ± obstruction ± infection.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Obstructive pyelonephritis / pyonephrosis** – obstructing stone + infection → urosepsis + renal destruction. Key finding: fever ≥38°C + rigors + unilateral flank pain; urine dip: nitrite+ / leukocyte+; lactate rising. **THIS is the emergency – needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy or stent) not just antibiotics.**
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)** – can present with flank/back pain mimicking renal colic. Key finding: pulsatile abdominal mass + hypotension + shock; no haematuria; pain radiates to back. **If >65y or vascular risk, do bedside US or CT aorta before assuming stone.**
- **Bilateral obstructing stones / solitary kidney with anuria** – complete blockage → hyperkalaemia, acidosis, acute kidney injury. Key finding: anuria + rising creatinine + no visible stone on CT? Actually, CT will show obstruction. Urgent decompression needed to prevent permanent loss of function.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Acute onset severe unilateral flank/loin pain radiating to groin/testis/labia; patient restless, unable to sit still; nausea/vomiting; urgency/frequency/dysuria if stone near vesicoureteric junction; macroscopic haematuria.
- **Key exam findings:** Patient in distress, writhing; costovertebral angle tenderness; no peritonism; may have low-grade fever (<38°C) from inflammation; if fever >38°C, suspect infection.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Renal colic (ureteric stone)** – classic radiation, haematuria, CT confirms stone.
- **Pyelonephritis** – fever >38.5°C, rigors, positive urine culture, bilateral symptoms less common; imaging may show hydronephrosis but no stone.
- **Ruptured AAA** – older age, vascular risk factors, pulsatile mass, hypovolaemic shock, no haematuria.
- **Testicular torsion** – scrotal pain with absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis; testicular ultrasound.
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest or back pain, pulse deficit, hypertension, widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Diverticulitis** – left lower quadrant pain, fever, change in bowel habit; CT shows diverticular inflammation.
- **Biliary colic/cholecystitis** – right upper quadrant pain, Murphy's sign, gallstones on ultrasound.
- **Appendicitis** – right iliac fossa pain, migration, anorexia; CT shows enlarged appendix.
- **Musculoskeletal back pain** – no autonomic features, no radiation to groin, no haematuria; movement-exacerbated.
- **Splitter:** **Haematuria** – if present on dipstick, strongly favours stone; if absent, think AAA or musculoskeletal cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urine dipstick (blood, nitrite, leukocytes, pH); pregnancy test in women of childbearing age; ECG if atypical chest pain or elderly.
- **Bloods:** U&E (creatinine, eGFR), CRP, FBC, calcium, urate; lactate if sepsis suspected; blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT KUB (low-dose non-contrast) – gold standard; detects nearly all stones, measures stone size, identifies hydronephrosis.
  - **Alternative (pregnant or young female):** Ultrasound KUB + plain AXR – less sensitive but avoids radiation; can show hydronephrosis and stones >5mm on XR.
  - **Second-line:** CT with IV contrast if CT KUB inconclusive or need to assess collecting system anatomy.
- **Special tests:** Stone analysis if passed (infrared spectroscopy); 24h urine collection for metabolic workup in recurrent stones or high-risk patients.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No formal scoring system used in UK; clinical diagnosis + CT confirmation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in all women of childbearing age (changes imaging and management); **blood cultures** before antibiotics if febrile (urosepsis).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Severe pain: Morphine 5-10mg IV/IM (or [check local policy] IV paracetamol + NSAID if no contraindication) until comfortable.
  - Nausea: Ondansetron 4-8mg IV/IM / metoclopramide 10mg IV/IM.
  - IV fluids if dehydrated, vomiting, or oliguric: 0.9% sodium chloride 500mL-1L over 1-2h.
  - Check renal function; if normal and no infection, can plan discharge with oral analgesia and tamsulosin (if stone >5mm in distal ureter).
  - If febrile or signs of sepsis: start empirical antibiotics (broad spectrum: e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or gentamicin [check local policy] after blood cultures) and call urology for urgent decompression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Admit if: sepsis, AKI, solitary kidney, uncontrolled pain, bilateral stones, large stone (>10mm) unlikely to pass, or social issues. Manage pain with PRN IV/oral analgesia; continue IV fluids if needed; monitor urine output; arrange urology review for potential ureteroscopy or ESWL.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Metabolic workup if recurrent or risk factors (see below). Dietary advice: increase fluid intake to produce >2L urine/day; reduce salt, moderate protein, avoid high oxalate foods if calcium oxalate stones (spinach, rhubarb, nuts, chocolate). Specific therapy based on stone type (e.g., thiazide for hypercalciuria, allopurinol for uric acid stones).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h unless on fluid restriction; VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20mg SC daily (if eGFR >30 and weight >50kg; adjust if obese or CKD); NBM if planned for surgical intervention; observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; strict fluid balance chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Pain:** Ibuprofen 400-600mg PO QDS (max 2.4g/day) – preferred if no contraindication (renal impairment, GI bleed, hypovolaemia). Monitor renal function.
  - **Severe pain:** Morphine sulphate 5-10mg IV over 5 minutes, then titrate; or oral morphine solution 10-20mg 4-hourly PRN.
  - **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4-8mg PO/IV TDS (max 24mg/day); or Metoclopramide 10mg PO/IV TDS.
  - **Tamsulosin** 400mcg PO once daily (for stone expulsion in distal ureter stones >5mm; use for up to 28 days; caution in hypotension, cataract surgery).
  - **Empirical antibiotics (if infection):** Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS (adjust if creatinine clearance <30) +/- Gentamicin [check local policy] (once daily dosing, check levels according to local protocol).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs avoid if eGFR <30, active peptic ulcer, coagulopathy, hypovolaemia. Tamsulosin risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome; known orthostatic hypotension.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** NSAIDs contraindicated in significant CKD; morphine reduce dose if eGFR <30; tamsulosin no renal adjustment but caution if hepatic impairment; gentamicin requires therapeutic drug monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin** – missing levels before second dose can cause nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity. Always check trough level (pre-dose) for once-daily dosing. **Morphine** – giving IV opioid without checking respiratory rate (<12/min) or GCS (risk of respiratory depression). **NSAIDs** – prescribing in hypovolaemia or AKI (no urine output) precipitates acute tubular necrosis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Obstructive pyelonephritis / pyonephrosis (sepsis, shock)
  - Renal colic uncontrolled pain
  - Acute kidney injury (bilateral or solitary kidney)
  - Ureteric perforation or rupture (rare, from instrumentation or large stone)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hydronephrosis / renal atrophy (if prolonged untreated obstruction)
  - Recurrent stones (up to 50% recur within 5-10 years)
  - Chronic kidney disease (esp. staghorn calculi, recurrent obstruction, or infection)

### Prognosis
- Majority (>90%) of stones <5mm pass spontaneously within 4-6 weeks; 10-20% require intervention. Mortality rare except from sepsis (urosepsis carries 20-40% mortality if treatment delayed). Recurrence high without preventive measures.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send stone for analysis** if patient passes or retrieves it – directs long-term prevention.
- **In febrile patient with obstructing stone, call urology within 1 hour** for emergency decompression – do not wait for antibiotics to work.
- **Advise patients to strain urine** to catch stone for analysis and to confirm passage.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give NSAIDs if serum creatinine not checked** – reversible AKI from combined hypovolaemia + NSAID.
- **Do not discharge patient with fever or obstruction** – risk of rapidly progressing to septic shock.
- **Do not forget pregnancy test** – CT KUB is contraindicated in pregnancy; use ultrasound + limited KUB instead.
- **Do not give tamsulosin for stones <5mm** – no proven benefit; side effects outweigh.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Uncontrolled pain despite oral analgesia; vomiting causing dehydration; fever or rigors (infection); unable to pass urine (anuria); visible blood in urine with clots.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 1-2 weeks if stone not passed; urology clinic referral if stone >5mm, remains in situ >4 weeks, or patient has recurrent stones. Arrange imaging (KUB or ultrasound) to confirm stone passage in 4-6 weeks if not visible.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior/ICU if: **NEWS2 ≥5** or any single parameter scoring 3 (esp. respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, SBP <90, heart rate >130, GCS <13). **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with fever suggests sepsis. **Anuria >12 hours** – call urology for emergency decompression. **Creatinine rising >50% from baseline** or >300 µmol/L – consider AKI and need for nephrostomy. **Crash team** if cardiac arrest or unresponsive/sepsis with refractory hypotension.
